_meta.title: Threat Modeling — STRIDE, DREAD, attack trees

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the six STRIDE threat categories?
questions.explanation: The six STRIDE categories are Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, and Elevation of Privilege. 'Replication' is not a STRIDE category — it is a concept in distributed systems (data replication).

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a trust boundary in a Data Flow Diagram (DFD) used for threat modeling?
options: To separate different programming languages used in the system
To indicate where data moves from one security trust level to another
To mark the physical location of servers in a datacenter
To draw a line between frontend and backend code
questions.explanation: A trust boundary (drawn as a dashed line in DFDs) marks where data crosses from one trust level to another — e.g., from the public internet to an internal network. Every crossing is a point where security properties (authentication, encryption, access control) may change and should be examined for threats.

questions.question: When scoring a threat with DREAD, which category specifically addresses how easy it is for an attacker to reproduce the threat?
questions.explanation: Reproducibility (the 'R' in DREAD) measures how reliably an attacker can trigger the threat. A score of 1 means it is very hard (race condition, timing-dependent), 2 means complex multi-step, and 3 means it works reliably every time (one click, always succeeds).

questions.question: In an attack tree, what is the semantic difference between an OR node and an AND node?
options: OR nodes represent cheaper attacks; AND nodes represent expensive ones
OR nodes require all child conditions; AND nodes require any one child
OR nodes represent a choice (any child is sufficient); AND nodes require all children simultaneously
OR nodes are for network attacks; AND nodes are for physical attacks
questions.explanation: In attack trees, OR means logical disjunction — the parent is achievable if any one child is satisfied (the attacker can choose the easiest path). AND means logical conjunction — all children must be satisfied for the parent to be achievable (all sub-goals are necessary). This distinction is critical for correctly modeling whether an attacker needs multiple simultaneous conditions or just one path.

questions.question: What is the STRIDE threat model used for?
options: Systematically categorizing security threats into Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information disclosure, Denial of service, and Elevation of privilege
questions.explanation: STRIDE is a mnemonic for six threat categories: Spoofing identity, Tampering with data, Repudiation, Information disclosure, Denial of service, Elevation of privilege. It helps systematically analyze a system by asking 'what if an attacker could do X?' for each category.
